missions.title: How this module works (read this first)

beats.html: Welcome back, Future Builder! Last week you learned what AI is — that a regular machine follows rules, and AI learns from examples. Huge. This week we go bigger. You're going on 12 new Missions about what AI actually does out in the world: the amazing stuff — helping doctors, the planet, and scientists — and the tricky stuff — big questions about fairness, privacy, and who gets to decide. Finish a Mission, earn a badge, move on. Collect all 12, then a brand-new Big Mission where you build something good with real AI. Ready? Let's go. 🚀
You're back — and this week, AI leaps off the screen and into the real world.

Remember the toaster from last week? A toaster does the same thing every time. And the cook who tasted a thousand meals and can invent a brand-new dish? That's AI — it learns. You already know the single biggest idea in all of AI. Most grown-ups still can't explain it, and you can. Take a second to feel good about that.
Now here's the question for this whole week: if someone hands you the most powerful tool humans have ever built… what happens next?

Because that's what AI is. Not just a fun app that draws dragons — a tool so powerful it's starting to change big things: how doctors find sickness, how we power cities, how scientists solve mysteries that have been stuck for a hundred years. That's the exciting part, and we'll start there.

But a tool this powerful also comes with big choices. Who gets to use it? Is it fair? Is your private stuff safe? Who decides where it goes next? Those are some of the most important questions in the whole world right now — and grown-ups are figuring them out at the very same time you are. You're not too young for these questions. You're actually the perfect age, because you're going to grow up living inside the answers.
So this week you get to think like a real builder: not just "what can AI do?" but "what should we do with it — and who gets to choose?" Spoiler: part of the answer is you.
Let's start with a quick one. No wrong answers — we'll come back to it at the very end and see how your thinking grew.
And one fast warm-up to wake up your Module 1 brain — sort these into what AI does vs. what's still a person's job. Trust your gut!

beats.affirmation: Welcome back. You showed up curious again — that never stops being your superpower.
beats.nextUp: Mission 2: AI Is Already Out There

beats.html: AI is already woven through the whole world — not just your screen.
When you think of AI, you probably picture a chatbot or a phone. But AI is way bigger than your screen. It's already woven into the whole world around you, quietly doing jobs you never see.

Think about a single normal day. A doctor's computer helps spot a problem on an X-ray. A farmer's drone checks which plants need water. A weather system scans the skies and warns a whole city that a storm is coming. A city's traffic computers reroute thousands of cars around a jam at once. In a science lab, AI helps invent a brand-new medicine. You never tap a screen for any of it — but it's all AI, working out in the world right now.

Here's a way to picture it. A hundred years ago, electricity was a brand-new novelty — a single light bulb people gathered around to stare at. Then, bit by bit, it slipped behind everything: the lights, the fridge, the school, the hospital, the whole city. You don't even think about it anymore — you just flip a switch. AI is doing the same thing, right now, in your lifetime. It's moving from "wow, a chatbot!" to quietly running behind big parts of the world.

That's actually why this module matters. When something becomes that important and that common, it stops being just a cool toy and starts being something we all have to think about carefully — how it's used, and whether it's used well. So this week we're going to visit the big places AI is showing up: hospitals, the planet, jobs, and the giant questions that come with all of it.

🍿 Video break! A quick look at AI quietly helping all around us — maps, photos, translation, and more.
“How AI Works in Everyday Life” · Google · YouTube

beats.affirmation: You can now spot AI out in the wild, not just in apps. Eyes open, Future Builder. 🌍

beats.html: AI helps doctors spot what tired eyes might miss — like a metal detector for sickness.
Here's one of the most hopeful things AI does in the whole world: it helps doctors keep people healthy.

Remember Mission 5 from last week, when you taught a computer to see by finding patterns in pixels? The exact same trick is now helping doctors. A doctor can look at an X-ray or a scan, but they're human — they get tired, and some patterns are really, really faint. An AI that has studied millions of scans can quietly point and say, "Hey, look right here." It catches little patterns a tired human eye might miss. Earlier catches mean people get help sooner — and sometimes that's a very big deal.

Think of it like a metal detector for sickness. The metal detector doesn't dig up the treasure or decide what to do — it just beeps so the person knows exactly where to look. The AI beeps; the doctor looks; the doctor decides. That last part matters: the AI is the helper, never the boss. A real human doctor always makes the final call, because caring for a person takes judgment and kindness, and that's a human superpower.

And it gets even more amazing. To invent a new medicine, scientists used to test ideas one at a time, by hand, for years. AI can help test millions of ideas incredibly fast — like trying a million keys in a lock in the time it used to take to try a few. That's helping people work on diseases that have hurt families for a very, very long time. Some of the kids reading this might grow up to be the scientist — with AI as their helper — who finally cracks one of them.
So when people say "AI is going to change the world," this is a big part of what they mean. A faster, sharper helper for the people whose whole job is helping us.

beats.affirmation: You just learned how AI helps keep people healthy. That's a superpower worth cheering for. 🩺

beats.html: AI helps with giant jobs: clean energy, weather, and taking care of the planet.
Some problems are too big for any one person to solve alone — like taking care of our whole planet. Good news: this is another place AI is becoming an amazing helper.

Clean energy. Think about all the power a city uses — lights, trains, schools, homes. Some of it comes from wind and sun, which are clean but tricky, because the wind doesn't always blow and the sun goes down at night. AI helps figure out the smartest way to use that energy: store some for later, send some where it's needed right now, and waste as little as possible. It's like a super-careful helper making sure not a single bit of clean power gets thrown away.

Weather and safety. Remember how AI learns from tons of examples? Now imagine an AI that has studied a billion past storms. It gets really good at guessing what a storm will do next — which means warning people earlier so they can get somewhere safe. Earlier warnings save lives.

Giant science puzzles. Some mysteries are so huge that scientists were stuck on them for decades. Not long ago, an AI helped figure out the shapes of almost every tiny protein inside living things — a puzzle people had worked on for ages. Cracking puzzles like that helps us understand nature, grow better food, and invent new things.

Here's the big idea: AI is great at looking at enormous amounts of information and finding the smartest pattern — way more than a person could ever hold in their head. So for problems that are really about huge numbers — a whole city's power, a whole planet's weather — it's an incredible teammate. The humans still decide what we care about and what to do. The AI helps us do it better.

beats.affirmation: You just saw AI help take care of the whole planet. Earth thanks you. 🌎

beats.html: Code does the loop, AI does the judgment, humans handle the exceptions.
You've probably heard a grown-up wonder, "Is AI going to change jobs?" The honest answer is yes — and once you understand how, it's a lot less scary and a lot more interesting.
Here's the secret rule, and it's the heart of this whole camp:

**Code handles the loop. AI handles the judgment inside the loop. Humans handle the

exceptions.**

Let's unpack that with a real example. Imagine a job where someone has to read ten thousand customer emails a week. A computer loop can zip through all ten thousand. The AI can read each one and make a smart guess — "this one's a happy thank-you, this one's a problem." And when a weird, tricky, or sad one shows up that needs real care? That's an exception — and a human steps in to handle it with kindness and good judgment.

See what happened? The AI didn't replace the person. It took the boring, repeating part — reading the same kind of thing ten thousand times — so the human could spend their time on the part that actually needs a human: caring, deciding, creating. A dishwasher didn't end cooking. It freed the cook to cook more. AI is like that for lots of jobs.

It's true that some jobs will change, and that can feel uncertain — that's a real feeling, and it's okay to wonder about it. But here's the part that doesn't make the news: new tools also create brand-new jobs. Someone has to teach the AI, check the AI, and decide what it should and shouldn't do. (Hmm — who's getting really good at understanding AI right now? You.) When your parents were kids, "app maker" and "video creator" weren't jobs at all.
So the future of work isn't "robots take over." It's teams — humans and AI, each doing what they're best at, with the human as the captain making the calls that matter.

🍿 Video break! How AI is changing the way grown-ups work.
“Ethics & AI: Privacy & the Future of Work” · Code.org · YouTube

beats.affirmation: You just learned how grown-ups and AI work as a team. Captain's hat: earned. 🧢
beats.nextUp: Mission 6: Every Tool Can Help or Hurt

missions.title: Every Tool Can Help or Hurt

beats.html: A tool isn't good or bad — the choices we make with it are.

Okay — you've seen the amazing side: AI helping doctors, the planet, and people at work. Now we turn a corner. Because the most powerful tool in the world comes with the most important choices in the world, and a smart builder thinks about both.
Here's the key idea, and it's small enough to fit in your pocket: a tool isn't good or bad by itself. The choices people make with it are what matter.

Think about a hammer. A hammer can build a cozy treehouse… or smash a window. Same hammer. The difference is the person holding it and what they choose to do. Think about fire: it cooks your dinner and keeps you warm, or it burns things down. The fire isn't good or evil — it depends on how it's used.

AI is exactly the same, just way more powerful — which means the choices matter way more. The same AI that recommends you a perfect new book could, if someone set it up that way, only ever show you one kind of thing and never let you discover anything new. The same AI that helps a doctor could be used carelessly somewhere it shouldn't be. Same tool — totally different outcome — depending on the choices.
That's why, from here on, we don't just ask "What can AI do?" We ask two more grown-up questions:

1. Should we? Just because we can do something doesn't always mean it's a good idea.
  2. Who gets to choose? Who decides how this powerful tool gets used — and is that fair?

You don't need to be scared of these questions. They're actually the exciting part, because they're where humans stay in charge of the future. For the next few Missions, you get to be a "help or hurt" detective — and you'll be surprised how good you already are at it.

🍿 Video break! Why being careful and responsible with AI really matters.
“Why AI Matters” · Code.org · YouTube

beats.affirmation: You just leveled up from "what can it do?" to "what should we do?" That's wisdom. 🛠️

beats.html: Your information is like your belongings: you get a say in who sees it.
Time for your first big builder question, and it's one you can actually use today: privacy.

Remember how AI gets smart? It learns from data — tons of examples. Here's the thing: you make data all day long, just by living your life. What you watch, what you search, where you go, what you type, what time you wake up. Little trails of information, everywhere you go online. Grown-ups have a name for that trail: your digital footprint.

Some of that is genuinely helpful! It's why an app can remember your favorite show or pick a song you'll love. That's the nice side. But it's also worth being thoughtful about, because your information is a little like your belongings — and you should get a say in who sees it.

Here's a way to feel the difference. You have a diary and you have a class bulletin board. Some things you happily pin to the bulletin board for everyone — your favorite color, a drawing you're proud of. Other things stay in the diary, just for you and people you trust — your home address, your passwords, where you'll be on Saturday. Smart people aren't scared of this; they're just thoughtful about which is which.
So here's your privacy power, three simple moves:

1. Think before you share. Ask, "Is this a bulletin-board thing or a diary thing?"
  2. Keep the diary stuff private — your full name, address, phone, passwords, where you are. Those are yours.
  3. When you're not sure, ask a grown-up you trust. That's not babyish — that's exactly what smart people do.
This isn't about being scared of the internet. It's about being the boss of your own information — which, after Module 1, you already know is the whole vibe here. You're in charge.

🍿 Video break! The trail of info you leave online — your digital footprint.
“The Digital Footprint” · Common Sense Education · YouTube

beats.affirmation: You're now the boss of your own information. Diary stuff stays in the diary. 🔒
beats.nextUp: Mission 8: Who Holds the Biggest Tools?

beats.html: What if one kid owned every ball at recess? Who holds the biggest tools matters.
Here's a question that even a lot of grown-ups haven't stopped to think about: the most powerful AI is really, really expensive and hard to build. So who actually has it?

Picture recess. Imagine one kid showed up owning every single ball, every jump rope, and the whole field. Not because they're mean — maybe they're perfectly nice! But if everybody else has to come ask that one kid every time they want to play… that's a little bit uncomfortable, right? Even with a nice kid, it just doesn't feel fair for one person to hold all of it.

Right now, the strongest AI tools in the world are a bit like that. Building a giant, powerful AI takes enormous computers and a lot of money, so only a few big companies and a few countries have the very biggest ones. That's not a movie villain thing — it's just how expensive and new this is. But it raises a real fairness question, and you're old enough to ask it: if a tool can shape the whole world, is it okay for only a few groups to hold the biggest ones?

Why does it matter? Because tools shape what the world becomes. Whoever holds the most powerful tools has a lot of say in what gets built and who it's built for. So lots of people think it's important that AI doesn't stay locked up with just a few — that more people, in more places, get to use it, learn it, and have a voice in it. (People like you, learning it right now, are part of spreading it out.)
There's no bad guy in this Mission. There's just a fairness question worth caring about: the biggest tools should help everybody, not just whoever happens to own the field.

🍿 Video break! Why fair access to powerful AI tools matters for everyone.
“Ethics & AI: Equal Access and Algorithmic Bias” · Code.org · YouTube

beats.affirmation: You just thought about fairness on a world-sized scale. That's big-kid thinking. ⚖️

beats.html: A flashlight lights your way home — or gets shined in your face. Same tool, different choice.
This Mission has the biggest idea in the whole module, and it builds right on the last one. We're going to take it slow and start somewhere comfy.

Good rules help everybody. Think about a traffic light, or taking turns on the swings, or raising your hand. Rules can be kind — they keep people safe and make sure everybody gets a fair turn. A world with zero rules would actually be scary. So rules aren't the bad guy here. Hold onto that.
Now the bigger idea. A super-powerful tool can be used to help people… or it can be used to boss people around. Same tool, very different choice — just like the hammer.

Here's the friendly way to picture it: a flashlight. A flashlight can help you find your way home in the dark — that's helping. Or someone could shine it right in your face to push you around — that's bossing. Same flashlight. The difference is the choice of the person holding it.

Powerful AI is the same. Used one way, it helps a city save energy, helps a doctor, helps you learn. Used another way, a powerful tool could be used to watch people all the time, or to control what they're allowed to do or say. In some places in the world, powerful tools do get used to boss people around instead of to help them.

Now — that's not here to scare you. (If it feels like a big thought, that's okay; big thoughts are allowed, and you can always talk about it with a grown-up you trust.) It's here because it's the most important reason people care so much about who controls AI and how it's used. This is exactly why the questions from the last few Missions matter: should we? and who gets to choose? The answer the world is working toward is this: good rules, and lots of different people having a say, keep powerful tools on the helping side.

And that's a hopeful ending, not a scary one. Tools don't decide how they're used — people do. Rules, fairness, and many voices are how we keep the flashlight pointed at the path home. And your generation gets to be some of those voices.

beats.affirmation: You just thought carefully about one of the hardest questions there is — and stayed hopeful. That's real courage. 🔦
beats.nextUp: Mission 10: Who Decides the Future of AI?

missions.title: Who Decides the Future of AI?

beats.html: Nobody decides alone — the future of AI is steered by lots of voices, including yours.

After the last couple of Missions, you might be thinking: okay, this is a super powerful tool, and how it's used really matters… so who's actually in charge of all this? Brilliant question. Let's answer it — and the answer is full of hope.
Here's the big one: nobody should decide alone. Not one company. Not one ruler. Not even one country. Something this powerful and this important is meant to be steered by lots of people, together.
How do people steer something big and shared? The same ways humans have always made big group decisions:

• They make rules together — agreements about what's okay and what's not.
  • Grown-ups vote for leaders who help make those rules.
  • People speak up — they say what they think is fair, and that actually changes things.
  • Some grown-ups have a whole job of keeping powerful tools safe and fair for everybody.
  • And new builders — hi, that's you — bring fresh ideas nobody older even thought of.

Think about the rules of a big playground game that everyone plays. The game works best when everyone helps agree on the rules — not when one kid makes them all up and bosses everyone around (you just learned why that's no fun in Mission 8!). The future of AI is like that game. It works best when lots of voices help shape it.

And here's the part that should make you sit up: the future of AI isn't decided yet. It's being figured out right now, while you're a kid — which means your voice is part of it. What you think is fair, what you'd build, what rules you'd want — that all counts. Most kids never realize they get a say in something this big. You just did.

🍿 Video break! Big ideas about how people get to shape what AI does next.
“How Will AI Change the World?” · TED-Ed · YouTube

beats.affirmation: You just put a rule for AI on the wall. That's you, helping steer the future. 🗳️
beats.nextUp: Mission 11: You Get to Help Build It

missions.title: You Get to Help Build It

beats.html: You don't just use AI — you get to help build it: curiosity + judgment + kindness.

Let's pull this whole week together, because you've come a long way. You learned that AI is powerful and already changing the world (Missions 2–5). You learned it comes with real choices about fairness, privacy, power, and control (Missions 6–10). So here's the big finish: *the people who get to shape all of this are the kids growing up with it. That's you.*

You're not just going to use AI. You get to help build it and guide it. And here's the secret about what makes a good builder — it's not just being clever with the tool. A great builder needs three things:

• Curiosity — to wonder, to ask "what if?", to keep trying. (You've had this since Mission 1.)
  • Judgment — to ask the grown-up questions: Should we? Is this fair? Who could this help or hurt? (You practiced this all module.)
  • Kindness — to build things that are good for people, not just cool or powerful.

That's the whole secret of this camp, actually. The most powerful thing in the future isn't the AI — it's a curious, careful, kind human deciding what to do with it. A robot can sort a million photos, but only a person can decide which idea is worth building, who it should help, and what's fair. AI brings the speed; you bring the wisdom. Together, that's an unstoppable team — with you as the captain.

So get curious. Ask the big questions. Dream up wild ideas — and ask if they're fair and kind before you build them. The future isn't something that just happens to you. You get to help build it — and now you know how to build it well.

🍿 Video break! You can be a builder of technology — not just a user of it.
“What Most Schools Don't Teach” · Code.org · YouTube

beats.affirmation: You wrote your own Builder's Code. That's the kind of person the future needs. 🌟

beats.html: You did it — Level 2. Look how big you can think about AI now.

Look how big your thinking got this week. Twelve Missions ago, AI was a tool that draws dragons. Now you can talk about how it's changing medicine, the planet, and jobs — and you can think clearly about fairness, privacy, power, and who gets to decide. That's not just "knowing about AI." That's wisdom about AI. Let's pull it all together.

The Big Ideas you unlocked this module: - AI is already out there, changing big parts of the world — hospitals, the planet, science, and jobs (Missions 2–5). - The work rule: code does the loop, AI does the judgment, humans handle the exceptions (Mission 5). - A tool isn't good or bad — the choices are (the hammer, Mission 6). - Your information is yours — be the boss of your privacy (Mission 7). - The biggest tools are held by a few today, and fairness means sharing the power (Missions 8–9). - Nobody decides alone — the future of AI is steered by lots of people, including you (Mission 10). - A great builder = curiosity + judgment + kindness (Mission 11).

Here's your challenge: tonight, teach one of these ideas to a grown-up. Maybe the hammer rule, maybe the recess-ball fairness idea, maybe "code does the loop." If you can teach it, you really own it — and you might just teach them something brand new.
Then take the quiz, look back at what you said about AI in Mission 1, and get ready — because now you get to build something good with real AI in the Big Mission.

missions.title: Build an AI Helper for the World

beats.html:
🚀 The Big Mission

Build an AI Helper for the World

You earned all 12 badges. Time for the best part — build something good with a real AI, then check it like a true builder.

Start the Big Mission →
